Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Fig. 2f and text describe CNR6 as 'a gene annotated as a cell number regulator (CNR6)'.

Source_Species.reason: All mutant and transgenic wheat in the study are in wheat background.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Methods state all wheat materials were in Triticum turgidum background.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Methods specify the tetraploid wheat ecotype Kronos.

Applied_Species.reason: CNR6 expression was measured in wheat plants/seedlings.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied wheat material is Triticum turgidum per Methods.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The applied material is Kronos background.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: CNR6 is tested in relation to JA status: reduced in low-JA tgw1, induced by JA, suppressed by JA inhibitor.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: JA is a phytohormone and oxylipin; the paper explicitly treats JA as a plant hormone and oxidized lipid.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: JA content was measured in leaves of WT and tgw1 (Fig. 2e), and CNR6 responses were assayed in seedling leaves (Fig. 2g,h).

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper links JA to regulation of CNR6 expression and organ growth-related traits such as leaf area and grain weight, though indirectly.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The paper supports positive association of CNR6 expression with JA status and discusses possible contribution to cell number/leaf area, but indirect contribution to grain weight is inferential and not directly tested for CNR6.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Results include qRT-PCR of CNR6 in tgw1 and KAT-2B overexpression lines, plus exogenous JA and DIEC treatments (Fig. 2f-h, Fig. 3d).

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: CNR6 was measured in seedling leaves upon JA/DIEC treatment and was lower in tgw1, higher after JA treatment.

Omics_Data.reason: PRJNA673987 is the study RNA-seq accession, but the paper does not explicitly state CNR6 itself came from RNA-seq; CNR6 expression evidence shown is qRT-PCR/targeted assays.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Fig. 2g,h and Methods specify 10 μM JA and 200 μM DIEC treatments.

Other_Important_Info.reason: This accurately reflects the authors' interpretation: CNR6 is JA-inducible and may positively regulate cell number in wheat, but no direct genetic proof for grain weight is provided.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not present CNR6 as a breeding target; this is a cautious extrapolation beyond direct evidence.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three points are consistent with Results: decreased in tgw1, induced by JA/suppressed by DIEC, and elevated in KAT-2B OE with larger leaf area correlation.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The paper explicitly calls SAG3 'a wheat leaf senescence-associated marker gene' (Fig. 5h text).

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Methods state all wheat materials are Triticum turgidum.

Applied_Species.reason: SAG3 expression was tested in wheat seedlings/mutants.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied species is Triticum turgidum per Methods.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: SAG3 is analyzed downstream of ABA; ABA content increased in tgw1 and ABA treatment induced SAG3.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: ABA is a phytohormone; it is derived from carotenoids, consistent with apocarotenoid classification though that term is not used explicitly.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: ABA was quantified from relevant wheat tissues and the chlorophyll/senescence context is leaves; Fig. 5 focuses on leaf-related phenotypes and seedling ABA treatment.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper links ABA increase with SAG3 induction and senescence/chlorophyll reduction.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results state increased ABA and SAG3 in tgw1, propose early senescence link to reduced chlorophyll, and connect reduced source capacity to grain-weight phenotype.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: qRT-PCR in mutant and exogenous ABA treatment are explicitly shown (Fig. 5h,i).

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Methods say young seedlings were treated with ABA; Fig. 5h,i show SAG3 elevated in tgw1 and induced by ABA.

Omics_Data.reason: While PRJNA673987 is the study RNA-seq accession, SAG3 evidence presented in Results is qRT-PCR; SAG3 is not explicitly listed as an RNA-seq hit in the paper text/table.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Higher SAG3 is linked by the authors to early senescence and reduced chlorophyll content, which is unfavorable for source capacity.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Methods and Fig. 5i specify 80 μM ABA treatment.

Other_Important_Info.reason: This matches the paper's framing of SAG3 as a downstream marker linking ABA to chlorophyll reduction, not as the primary causal metabolic gene.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not discuss SAG3 as a diagnostic marker or engineering target; this is an external interpretation.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each statement matches Results: SAG3 increased in tgw1, induced by ABA, and proposed to mark early senescence contributing to reduced chlorophyll/source capacity.

corrections.old_value: Primarily a diagnostic marker of ABA-related senescence, not yet a validated engineering target in this study
corrections.reason: The paper does not discuss SAG3 as a diagnostic marker or engineering target; this is an external interpretation.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Both TraesCS6B02G432600 and TraesCS6B01G432600 appear in the paper for KAT-2B, reflecting inconsistent assembly nomenclature; the combined field is not explicitly presented as such.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper identifies KAT-2B as keto-acyl thiolase 2B / 3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase-like gene.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number 2.3.1.16 is stated in the paper.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: The gene is annotated as KAT-2 / 3-ketoacyl-CoA thiolase-like, i.e., thiolase family.

Source_Species.reason: The study gene is from wheat.

Applied_Species.reason: The main functional tests were performed in wheat, though Arabidopsis complementation was also used.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Applied wheat species is Triticum turgidum.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: The paper directly states KAT-2B is involved in β-oxidation during JA synthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: Results place KAT-2B in the peroxisomal β-oxidation step of JA biosynthesis.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The paper supports that it is an internal β-oxidation step, but does not use the term 'midstream'.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper does not establish rate-limiting status, but does support KAT2 as an essential step in β-oxidation/JA production.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: JA is the metabolite shown reduced in tgw1 and elevated in overexpression line.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: JA is described as a plant hormone and discussed among oxidized lipids.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: JA content was measured in leaves/seedling leaves (Fig. 2e, Fig. 3c).

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper links KAT-2B/JA to grain, leaf, and pollen development and grain weight determination.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Loss-of-function lowers JA and grain weight; overexpression raises JA, grain width/weight, TKW, and yield metrics in field trials.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Acetoacetyl CoA and CoA are explicitly used in the recombinant enzyme assay; broader 3-ketoacyl-CoA intermediates are consistent with the enzyme's described β-oxidation role.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper states KAT2 produces acetyl CoA during β-oxidation, but the exact products 'shortened acyl-CoA plus acetyl-CoA' are not directly measured in this study.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: This description matches the paper's statements that KAT2 shortens carbon chains in β-oxidation and produces acetyl CoA, supporting JA biosynthesis.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: The paper directly supports KAT-2B as supplying an essential β-oxidation step for JA biosynthesis.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: All listed methods are reported: BSA/mapping, wheat OE, complementation of tgw1, enzyme assay, localization, Arabidopsis kat2 rescue, and JA profiling.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Results/figures state KAT-2B is highly expressed in most tissues except embryo and higher than KAT-2A across tissues/stages.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Same evidence as above in wheat source species.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: GFP-KAT-2B co-localized with a peroxisome marker.

Omics_Data.reason: The paper explicitly cites RNA-seq accession PRJNA673987, public microarray GSE12508, and Triticeae Multi-omics Center; KAT-2B expression analyses used these resources.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods specify maize ubiquitin promoter for overexpression/complementation constructs and CaMV 35S promoter for GFP localization.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: Field trials in 2019/2020 are explicit; one OE line showed JA increase after pathogen treatment (Supplementary Fig. 6); JA/DIEC/ABA treatments were used for downstream analyses.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results explicitly note reduced GNS in OE lines, reflecting GN–GW trade-off.

Other_Important_Info.reason: All listed details are in the paper: Q364* truncation removing downstream active residues, residual KAT-2A likely in mutant, and KAT-2A/B rescue Arabidopsis kat2.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper emphasizes yield increase in OE lines and proposes precise genome engineering of KAT-2B/JA biosynthesis for wheat improvement.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summary points are directly supported by the Results and Conclusion.

corrections.reason: No EC number 2.3.1.16 is stated in the paper.
